question: Match each type of network technology with its description.

pairs.right: Operates over a small geographical area, such as a college campus
Offered by phone companies and uses twisted-pair cabling to the home
Offered by TV companies and uses coax cabling to the home
Dedicated, high-performance network that moves data between servers and storage

question: Match each of the following terms with its definition.

pairs.left: A device that forwards packets based on destination IP address
A device that forwards frames based on destination MAC address
A set of devices connected by some form of transmission medium
A chunk of data that is sent on a network
A set of rules that govern how devices on a network exchange data

question: Which of the following is an enterprise WAN technology used by large organizations that need lots of bandwidth?
answer: Optical Transport Network

question: Match each layer in the Internet protocol stack with its description.

pairs.right: Sends bits across the wire (or wireless medium)
Sends data to a neighboring device such as a switch
Forwards data through an internetwork and runs routing protocols
Interacts with the network user; provides client/server communications

question: Drag the correct layer of the Internet protocol stack to each protocol.
Which two layers are present in the OSI model but not in the Internet Protocol model?
answer: Presentation and Session

question: Why do we use a layered model for networking?
choices: ICANN says that networks must be layered for participation in the Internet.
Layering makes it easier to update system components.
A layer can duplicate lower-layer functionality.
Packets can be shorter with fewer headers when layering is used.
answer: Layering makes it easier to update system components.

question: What does encapsulation refer to in the networking field?
choices: Adding a layer's header and (optionally) trailer to a protocol message passed down to the layer from an upper layer
Encoding data in the proper format based on the standard protocol rules for a layer
Hiding protocol data so that unauthorized entities can't decipher the data without a key
Layering network functionality in order to modularize and abstract network tasks
answer: Adding a layer's header and (optionally) trailer to a protocol message passed down to the layer from an upper layer

question: What is a broadcast address?
choices: A destination address in a packet that means the packet goes to every node
A source address in a packet that means the packet comes from a broadcast
An address that identifies a specific network node
A type of address used exclusively on broadband networks
answer: A destination address in a packet that means the packet goes to every node

question: What is the middle window pane (shown here) in Wireshark called?
answer: The Packet Details pane

question: Match each layer in the Internet protocol stack with its description.

pairs.right: Sends bits across the wire (or wireless medium)
Sends data to a neighboring device such as a switch
Forwards data through an internetwork and runs routing protocols
Interacts with the network user; provides client/server communications

question: Which of the following best describes the Internet?
choices: A system of centralized mainframe servers
A single network owned by one company
A collection of networks connected together
answer: A collection of networks connected together

question: What is the main purpose of the traceroute (or tracert) tool?
choices: To assign IP addresses to devices on a network
To check end-to-end delay and show the path packets take
To measure the available bandwidth on a link
To encrypt data during network transmission
answer: To check end-to-end delay and show the path packets take

question: Why is twisted-pair cabling twisted?
choices: The cabling had a difficult childhood.
The cabling needs the twists to make it stronger.
Twisting the cables helps cancel out noise.
Twisting the cables decreases the signal-to-noise ratio.
So it can react to changes more quickly.
answer: Twisting the cables helps cancel out noise.

question: Match each advantage of copper cabling with its description.

pairs.right: Can be drawn into thin wires without breaking

question: Which category of UTP cable is used for Gigabit Ethernet?
answer: Category 6

question: Which of the following is an accurate advantage of copper cabling compared to fiber-optic cabling?
choices: Copper cabling is susceptible to electromagnetic interference.
Copper cabling is susceptible to attenuation.
Copper cabling supports higher bandwidth than fiber-optic cabling.
Copper cabling is less expensive than fiber-optic cabling, especially for short distances.
Copper is more plentiful on the earth than glass.
answer: Copper cabling is less expensive than fiber-optic cabling, especially for short distances.

question: Which technology supports the highest bandwidth?
answer: Singlemode fiber

question: What is an advantage of fiber-optic cabling?
choices: It is immune to electromagnetic noise
It is widely used in homes and enterprises
answer: It is immune to electromagnetic noise

question: What is the most common network medium used for Ethernet?
answer: Unshielded twisted-pair copper cable

question: Which of the following best describes full-duplex data flow?
choices: Data flows on a single cable that is terminated at both ends.
Data can flow in only one direction.
Data can flow in only one direction at a time.
Data can flow in both directions at a time.
Data is blocked from flowing in any direction.
answer: Data can flow in both directions at a time.

question: Which of the following types of cabling is usually used in Ethernet LANs to connect end-user computers?
answer: Unshielded Twisted Pair (UTP)

question: Which protocol would you use to implement a redundant Layer 2 switched network that has multiple possible paths between nodes but only one active path?
answer: Spanning Tree Protocol (STP)

question: What are the names of the fields in an Ethernet II frame?
Where is the Link Layer typically implemented on an end system?
choices: All of the answers are correct
answer: All of the answers are correct

question: What do the first six hexadecimal numbers in a MAC address represent?
answer: Vendor code

question: How long are MAC addresses in bits?
Drag the correct layer of the Internet protocol stack to each protocol.
Match each definition of a wireless technology with the term that it defines.

pairs.right: Multiple antennas are used at the transmitter and receiver.
Devices communicate in a peer-to-peer network without a central access point.
Devices communicate with each other through a base station or access point.
Wireless clients listen for Beacon frames to learn about nearby access points.
Wireless clients send Probe Requests that contain a Service Set Identifier (SSID).

question: Match each wireless networking security technology with its definition.

pairs.right: The most secure way to do wireless networking for business employees because data is sent in an encrypted tunnel
Provides security by requiring the user to know the SSID
Implements security by allowing only some devices to use the network based on their unique hardware identifier
Encrypts data using the RC4 stream cipher but is easily cracked
Encrypts data with the Advanced Encryption Standard (AES)

question: What type of communication does Wi-Fi use to transmit and receive?
answer: Half-duplex

question: What is the fundamental job of a wireless base station?
choices: Relay packets from wireless hosts to a wired network
Organize wireless nodes into an ad hoc network
Increase the Signal to Noise Ratio (SNR)
Reduce interference caused by hidden wireless nodes
All of the answers are correct.
answer: Relay packets from wireless hosts to a wired network

question: Which IEEE standard defines Wireless LAN (Wi-Fi) technologies?
answer: IEEE 802.11

question: What is the media access control protocol used on wireless LANs?
choices: Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance (CSMA/CA)
All of the answers are correct.
answer: Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance (CSMA/CA)

question: What is the hidden node problem?
choices: A problem in wireless networking that occurs when a node is visible to some nodes but not to all.
A security problem caused by nodes secretly encrypting their data.
A problem at the IP layer where routers forward traffic without telling other routers.
An HTML tag that hides items, thus making debugging difficult.
answer: A problem in wireless networking that occurs when a node is visible to some nodes but not to all.

question: Which statement about WPA2 Enterprise is true?
choices: It uses a pre-shared key that is manually configured on all clients
It relies on TKIP for encryption
It typically uses 802.1X with a RADIUS server for authentication
It can't provide protection against rogue access points
answer: It typically uses 802.1X with a RADIUS server for authentication

question: Match each of the following terms with its definition.

pairs.right: A device that forwards packets in the core of a network
A network that operates within a limited geographic area
A set of devices connected by some form of transmission medium
A chunk of data that is sent on a network
A set of rules that govern how devices on a network exchange data

question: Match each type of network technology with its description.

pairs.right: An internetwork that operates over a large geographic area
Dedicated digital, copper circuits that a carrier provides for a customer
Offered by phone companies and uses twisted-pair cabling to the home
Offered by TV companies and uses coax cabling to the home
Dedicated, high-performance network that moves data between servers and storage

question: What information should always accompany an IPv4 address in addressing documentation?
choices: An indication of the prefix length, either as a subnet mask or as /Length in CIDR format
An indication of the domain name (FQDN)
The address of the DHCP server
The address of the NAT gateway
answer: An indication of the prefix length, either as a subnet mask or as /Length in CIDR format

question: In a Unix-based traceroute capture, what does Frame 26 indicate?

pairs.right: To learn the path to the destination (e.g., 72.163.4.185)

question: A Wireshark capture shows a Juniper device using ARP. Which statement is true?
choices: The device is using ARP to find the MAC address for a specific IP (e.g., 66.241.68.16).
The MAC address for the target is always 00:00:00:00:00:00.
The packet is sent to the entire Internet.
answer: The device is using ARP to find the MAC address for a specific IP (e.g., 66.241.68.16).

question: What is the main reason that 802.11 uses CSMA/CA instead of CSMA/CD?
choices: Wireless signals travel faster than wired signals
Collision detection is difficult in a wireless environment
answer: Collision detection is difficult in a wireless environment

question: What information does a router use to direct a packet to its destination?
answer: Destination IP address

question: Why does a router have more than one IP address?
choices: A router has an IP address on each of the networks it connects.
answer: A router has an IP address on each of the networks it connects.

question: What are the RFC 1918 private IP address ranges?
answer: All of the addresses listed

question: In Wireshark, what does a device address like fe80::aa20:66ff:fe58:4f69 refer to?
answer: An IPv6 address

question: Subnet Analysis (172.16.20.10 with mask 255.255.255.0)
Subnet Analysis (10.1.1.1 with mask 255.255.240.0)
Subnet Analysis (66.241.68.17/27)
iPhone Wireless Network Analysis
Why do routers run a routing protocol?
To learn about other networks and how to reach them
To determine an IP address for their interfaces
To assign IP addresses to their clients
To learn MAC addresses for connected devices

answer: To learn about other networks and how to reach them

question: Match the characteristic with the routing type (Distance-vector vs. Link-state).

pairs.left: Maintains a routing table of networks, direction, and distance
Periodically transmits the full routing table via broadcast
Sends updates only when an interconnection changes status
Treats the internetwork as a map or graph

question: What type of route is 0.0.0.0/0 via 192.168.10.1?
answer: Default

question: What is the address of the default gateway for the computer in the Windows route print screenshot?
What is typically found in a router's routing table?
choices: Destination address ranges and the interface to use to reach each range
Addresses of servers clients are trying to reach
Port numbers for Internet host services
answer: Destination address ranges and the interface to use to reach each range

question: Is the listed protocol Distance-vector or Link-state?
Based on the show ip route output provided, which statement is true?
choices: The output displays only connected and static routes.
The output is from Router B.
The output is from Router A and shows EIGRP routes.
answer: The output is from Router A and shows EIGRP routes.

question: Which of the following routing protocols is an inter-autonomous-system protocol?
answer: BGP (Border Gateway Protocol)

question: What is an autonomous system?
choices: A set of networks that are under the administrative control of a single entity
A router that acts independently from all other routers
A router that makes self-assigned decisions without configuration
An algorithm for sending routing information
answer: A set of networks that are under the administrative control of a single entity

question: What is a broadcast address?
choices: A destination address in a packet that means the packet goes to every node
A source address from a broadcast origin
An address that identifies a specific network node
A type of address used exclusively on broadband
answer: A destination address in a packet that means the packet goes to every node

question: Which type of routing protocols use the Bellman-Ford algorithm?
answer: Distance-vector

question: Which type of routing protocols use Dijkstra's algorithm?
answer: Link-state

question: Which term refers to the determining factor used by routing algorithms to decide which route is better?
answer: Metric

question: Which routing term refers to the closest adjacent router a packet can go through?
answer: Next hop

question: How do path-vector routing protocols recognize a routing loop?
choices: When a router receives a route update, if its own autonomous system number is in the path, there's a loop.
Updates hit the maximum hop count
The split horizon rule eliminates all loops
answer: When a router receives a route update, if its own autonomous system number is in the path, there's a loop.

question: Which of the following lists fields in the UDP header?
choices: Sender IP, destination IP, length, checksum
Source port, destination port, length, checksum
Sequence number, acknowledgement number, window size, checksum
answer: Source port, destination port, length, checksum

question: Match the well-known port number to the network service.
What process (or processes) does the screenshot show?
choices: All of the answers are correct
answer: All of the answers are correct

question: In the URL https://inside.sou.edu/cs/faculty.html, what does faculty.html refer to?
answer: A file name

question: Match each layer in the Internet protocol stack with its description.

pairs.right: Sends bits across the wire (or wireless medium)
Sends data to a neighboring device such as a switch
Forwards data through an internetwork and runs routing protocols
Interacts with the network user; provides client/server communications
